faq.a: pH stands for 'power of hydrogen.' It's a logarithmic scale (0–14) measuring acidity and alkalinity. 7 is neutral, below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic (alkaline).

faq.q: How do I calculate pH from hydrogen ion concentration?
faq.a: Use the formula pH = -log₁₀[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L. This calculator applies it automatically.

faq.q: What is the pH of pure water?
faq.a: Pure water at 25°C has a pH of 7 and [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ mol/L. It's neutral, with equal concentrations of H⁺ and OH⁻.
pH below 7 is acidic (more H⁺ ions). pH above 7 is basic or alkaline (fewer H⁺ ions). The lower the pH, the more acidic.

faq.q: Is pH 3 more acidic than pH 5?
faq.a: Yes—much more. The scale is logarithmic. pH 3 is 100 times more acidic than pH 5 (each unit = 10× difference).

faq.q: What are real-world examples of acidic and basic solutions?
faq.a: Acidic: lemon juice (pH 2), stomach acid (1.5–3.5), vinegar (2.4). Basic: baking soda (8.3), bleach (12–13), ammonia (11).
Yes, all our calculators are free and don't require registration or account creation.

faq.q: Can I use this for lab work or school?
faq.a: Yes, this is a quick reference and learning tool. For lab work, verify with lab-grade pH meters or indicators. Always consult your teacher or lab supervisor for accuracy requirements.
